quizzes.title: Bee Biology & Evolution Quiz
quizzes.description: Test your knowledge about bee evolution, social behavior, and unique adaptations.

questions.title: Which of the following bee species are considered social insects?
questions.explanation: Honey bees, bumblebees, and stingless bees live in hierarchical colonies. Mason bees, like over 90% of bee species, are solitary.

questions.title: All bees are herbivores, feeding on nectar and pollen.
questions.description: Decide whether this statement is true or false.
questions.explanation: Unlike wasps and ants (which are carnivorous/omnivorous), bees are exclusively herbivorous, consuming nectar for energy and pollen for protein.

questions.title: The immediate ancestors of bees were stinging wasps in the family Ammoplanidae.
questions.description: Fill in the blank with the correct family name.
questions.explanation: Phylogenetic studies indicate that bees evolved from predatory wasps of the family Ammoplanidae.

questions.title: Arrange these evolutionary milestones in chronological order, from earliest to most recent.
questions.description: Order the events based on the timeline provided in the text.
questions.explanation: Bees originated in the Early Cretaceous (~124 mya). All modern families diverged by the end of the Cretaceous (~66 mya). South American bees colonized North America by the end of the Cretaceous, and eusocial diversity flourished in the Eocene (~45 mya).
items: Divergence of all modern bee families (by end of Cretaceous)
Colonization of North America by South American bees (end of Cretaceous)

questions.title: Match each bee group to its characteristic.
questions.description: Draw a line from the bee group to the correct description.
questions.explanation: Honey bees build hives; sweat bees are the most common in the Northern Hemisphere; Megachile pluto is the largest bee; some stingless bees are extremely tiny.

pairs.right: Construct hexagonally celled waxy nests called hives
Most common bees in the Northern Hemisphere, often mistaken for wasps or flies
Largest species of bee, females up to 39 mm long
Workers less than 2 mm long in some species
